_comment: Type B — Hasty Generalization fallacy (Q38-style / record-breakers). Implicit false assumption about category non-overlap (population containment). High-confidence fallacy.
source_text: At our regional athletics competition last Saturday, ten students from our school qualified for the state finals. During the competition, six school records were broken. This shows that more than half of our student qualifiers must be record-breakers — a remarkable achievement for the school.

premises.text: Ten students from the school qualified for the state finals.
Six school records were broken during the competition.

assumptions.text: Each of the six broken records was broken by a different student (categories are non-overlapping).

conclusion.text: More than half (i.e. more than five) of the ten student qualifiers must be record-breakers.

fallacies.explanation: The argument moves from six records broken (P2) to 'more than five distinct students are record-breakers' (C1). This requires the implicit assumption A1 that each record was broken by a separate individual. In reality, one student could have broken multiple records, so as few as one student might account for all six. The count of records broken does not equal the count of distinct record-breaking students.
